content:
A Corpus-based Real-time Text Classification and Tagging Approach for Social Data

Atia Bano Memon1,2, Dileep Kumar Sootahar2, Kirshan Kumar Luhana2, Kyrill Meyer3,4*

1Department of Computer Science at the University of Leipzig, Germany

2Department of Computer Science at the University of Sindh, Pakistan

3Mittweida University of Applied Sciences, Mittweida, Germany

4Institute for Digital Technologies, Leipzig, Germany

Correspondence: 
Kyrill Meyer
kyrill.meyer@hs-mittweida.de

Keywords: social data1, text classification2, document tagging3, text matching4, social networking sites5, social data integration6, social data analysis7.

Abstract

With the rapid accumulation of large amounts of user-generated content through social media, social data reuse and integration has gained an increasing attention recently. This has made it almost obsolete for software applications to collect, store, and work with own data stored on local severs. Whilst, with the provision of Application Programming Interfaces from the leading social networking sites, the data acquisition and integration has become possible; the meaningful usage of such unstructured, non-uniform, and incoherent data collections need special procedures of data summarization, understanding, and visualization. One particular aspect in this regard that needs special attention are the procedures for data (text snippets in form of social media posts) categorization and concept tagging in order to filter out the relevant and most suitable data for the particular audience and for the particular purpose. In this regard, we propose a corpus-based approach for searching and successively categorizing and tagging the social data with relevant concepts in real-time. The proposed approach is capable of addressing the semantical and morphological similarities, and domain-specific vocabularies of query strings and tagged concepts. We demonstrate the feasibility and application of our proposed approach in a web-based tool that allows searching Facebook posts and provides search results together with a concept map for further navigation, filtering and refining of search results. The tool has been evaluated by performing multiple search queries and resultant concept maps and annotated texts are analyzed in terms of their precision. The approach is thereby found effective in achieving its stated goal of classifying text snippets in real-time.

Introduction

Nowadays, with the rapid introduction and advancement of social media platforms on one hand, and growing attention of user community towards digitalization and social networking on the other had; the world has transformed into a global village whereby everyone has a sort of digital presence and is actively participating in social networking activities . The services offered by social media platforms have expanded so well that they have provided their users with extensive possibilities for exchanging information in various fields such as education, health, culture, entertainment, sports and other domains of knowledge . As a result, with the passage of time a huge amount of user-generated data through social networking (i.e. social data) is being accumulated on social media servers . Social data is defined as large amounts of multi-structured user-generated data being collected at social network data servers as a result of user interactions within social networking and online community sites . This implies that every time a user tweets on Twitter/X, posts an update on Facebook or LinkedIn, records Check-ins on Foursquare, and shares videos on YouTube, photos on Instagram, and pins on Pinterest, either way they are voluntarily or involuntarily generating a significant amounts of social (meta) data. Research advocates that this social data, as being collected from user communities, has much potential to support third-party applications for specific audiences and specific purposes . The social platform users thus can be regarded as prosumers who produce and also consume user-generated data simultaneously. This is particularly important as social data can easily be categorized as its sibling concept of big data . Big data is categorized by its five properties, also referred as 5Vs of big data. This includes the variety (different forms of data), volume (huge amount of data), velocity (rapid speech of data accumulation), veracity (structured data), and value (importance of data). Social data (aka community data) also encompasses these features and is thus particularly referred as social big data or big social data  that constitutes about 95% of big data sources available in this decade .

Formally, social big data is defined as “any high-volume, high-velocity, high-variety and/or highly semantic data that is generated from technology-mediated social interactions and actions in digital realm, and which can be collected and analyzed to model social interactions and behavior” . This social big data offers numerous opportunities for the data researchers and analysts to re-use the already available data in different ways and generate various relevant insights for specific purposes . For example, students’ casual talks on these media can be helpful in understanding their experiences, opinions, feelings, and emotions about the learning and educational institutes . Similarly, users’ reviews and informal discussion over these media regarding a particular product or service can be helpful to understand the feedback and determine the acceptance or needs for improvement for the business organization. This provides a medium of direct conversation between customers and businesses and assists the businesses in course of various functionalities encompassing business intelligence such as personalized recommendation systems, opinion analysis, expertise retrieval, and computational advertising . Many studies have suggested the effectiveness and benefits of leveraging social big data in supporting various information retrieval and analysis tasks for the predictive analysis such as for political trending and forecasting or stock market prediction , opinion mining or sentimental analysis during any epidemic analysis , disaster relief  or crime identification , business intelligence as for targeted advertisement and marketing strategies , social network analysis for understanding the community and its features , and topic modeling and data analytics as in human machine interactions . According to  , utilizing social big data for such applications as opposed to the data collected through traditional media of communication is more favorable as 1) it offers convenience in assessing the information and expressed emotions, 2) it allows the insights to the situation from different perspectives and 



locations, 3) is collected through participation in an interactive, decentralized, uncontrolled, and large-scale discussions, and 4) is a cheap and efficient way to receive real-time updates in an unofficial and irregular manner.

Whilst, with the provision of Application Programming Interfaces from the leading social networking sites, the acquisition, integration, and re-use of user data has become possible ; the meaningful usage of such unstructured, non-uniform, and incoherent yet semantically rich data collections need special procedures of data integration and summarization, data analysis and understanding, knowledge representation, and data visualization . It is believed that social big data analysis is an interdisciplinary field spanning across various related fields. It draws upon the intersection of three major areas: social media as natural source of data and data analysis, big data as a parallel and massive processing paradigm, and data analysis algorithms and methods for extracting and analyzing the data . One particular aspect in this regard that needs special attention are the procedures for data (text snippets in form of social media posts) categorization and concept tagging in order to filter out the relevant and most suitable data for the particular audience and for the particular purpose. Given such large heterogeneous datasets, manual processing, organizing, and analysis tends to be very difficult, time consuming, less accurate and much expensive. Hence, drawing valuable and relevant knowledge from such data is a challenging task  which has not been completely solved by the research community yet. 

In this regard, this paper proposes a corpus-based approach for searching and successively categorizing and tagging the social data with relevant concepts in real-time. The proposed approach is capable of addressing the semantic and morphological similarities, and domain-specific vocabularies of query strings and tagged concepts. As a case based approach, the proposed approach is implemented in a web-based tool that allows searching Facebook posts and provides search results together with a concept map for further navigation, filtering and refining of search results. The tool has been evaluated by performing multiple search queries and resultant concept maps and annotated text segments are analyzed in terms of precision by the researchers adopting an expert evaluation method. The approach is thereby found effective in achieving its stated goal of classifying text snippets in real-time. As a conclusion, we contribute a novel approach for social data categorization that can be integrated in various data intensive applications that leverage textual social data and are adopted to different real life scenarios.

The remainder of the paper is structured as follows. Section 2 offers background information regarding different ways of text classification, and thereby describes the problem statement and research objectives of present paper. The research approach of current study is described in section 3. The proposed approach is presented in section 4 while the section 5 presents the case implementation and section 6 presents the evaluation results of the proposed approach. The paper concludes in section 7 with an outlook to future research agenda.

Background

Text classification and concept tagging has been focus of researchers for a long time (e.g. ) and has been studied in many real life applications such as news filtering and organization, information retrieval and management, opinion mining, and email classification and spam filtering, to name a few . Research on the analysis of structured data has somehow matured recently; however, the analysis of huge amounts of unstructured data which prevails in recent years still brings challenges for the researchers and application developers. Such data contain unrelated and diverse information and thereby offers no outline to identify any defined patterns or structural manifestations . Text classification, a task of natural language processing, refers to assigning categories to the textual data in order to facilitate its effective search, management, and navigation . Text classification often assigns multiple categories to individual pieces of text which might or might not be mutually exclusive. The essence of text classification is the process of adding tags or annotation to unstructured data for further analysis. Usually, text tagging can be achieved manually or automatically. However, manually tagging unstructured data can be difficult, ineffective, time consuming, and an expensive task . Therefore, the researchers have laid substantial efforts in devising the approaches for the automatic classification of unstructured data . This is particularly relevant whenever comprehensive human coding is not feasible due to the amount of data or because immediate classification information is required. Related work in information retrieval domain has focused on search engine fundamentals such as indexing and improved through feedback and query reformulation . While the recent work in this domain has focused on data mining and machine learning paradigms . The existing approaches can be categorized into three broad categories: rule-based approaches, machine learning based approaches, and hybrid approaches.

Rule-based text classification approaches

Rule-based text classification approaches consist of manually created rules that define the classification scheme. The rules are often described in terms of taxonomy (as a thesaurus, dictionary, or ontology) or folksonomy. The taxonomies are usually predefined, hierarchical in nature with top-down approach, and domain specific; whilst the folksonomy based approaches are non-hierarchical in nature with bottom-up approach that allow the users to tag their data with relevant terms that they find appropriate according to their content . When taxonomies exist which essentially offer a list of possible classes of data, there are various approaches suggested to determine the particular class of individual data . The major problem with taxonomy based techniques is that they might offer good accuracy but creating those taxonomies take a deep understanding and a lot of time. They require an authoritative source for their creation as well as validation. These methods also tend to be less scalable as new data might need new rules and they are difficult to code again with existing rules. Thus, with time they tend to produce more false-positives and false-negatives . In comparison, folksonomies are open-ended and agree more with human understanding . Folksonomies offer benefits of ease of implementation and reflection of intended user vocabulary; however, the problem with folksonomies is the uncontrolled vocabulary leading to the ambiguity due to the use of synonyms and noise tags by the users . Alternatively, rule-based approaches include the techniques to extract relevant keywords from the text itself through various statistical techniques such as Bag of Words, TF-IDF, N-grams, etc. as the textual features of the data . These approaches rely on word frequencies, and the keywords extracted therein are not always relevant and are not necessarily based on the concepts present in the text. An interesting approach in this regard was presented by   where they 



computed concepts based on word co-occurrences data which they claim to improve the performance of their text classification task.

Machine learning based text classification approaches

The recent research in the area of text classification has suggested various machine learning approaches that offer relatively higher accuracy and efficiency for large unstructured datasets. Machine language approaches in general involve building classifiers from different features which are then trained for automatic classification from instances of already labeled data. The machine learning approaches are classified into three types based on how they develop and train the classifiers; i.e. supervised, semi-supervised, and unsupervised approaches . Supervised learning based techniques explore various features of given content and find a suitable set of features through binary classification. The training data is labeled through human annotators who manually interpret and categorize the training data . The most common algorithms of supervised learning applied for text classification are based on Support Vector Machine , decision tree , k-Nearest-Neighbors , and other binary classification techniques. While supervised learning approaches have achieved good performance ; the major drawback of such approaches is that their performance is highly dependent on the training data and thus they require huge amount of labeled data for the training of classifiers. Also, labeling data for training by the human annotators is also time consuming and expensive process. Therefore, these approaches are not suitable for the domains where standard labeled datasets are not available. The high dependence on training data also makes the classifiers difficult to adapt to new settings and real-world applications . Alternatively, there are unsupervised learning techniques that do not require any labeled data. Unsupervised learning algorithms determine how data can be organized into different groups called clusters. This involves learning from unlabeled data to classify new inputs. For example,   suggested an unsupervised approach based on self-organizing maps to cluster the social data containing bully traces. Likewise,   used K-means algorithms to identify users’ behavior and classify them accordingly. Unsupervised learning is useful when labeled data is not accessible; however, the performance is not always good. On the merge of supervised and unsupervised techniques are the semi-supervised approaches for text classification. The semi-supervised algorithms leverage mixture of labeled data (as in supervised) and unlabeled data (as in unsupervised) to build learning models. Semi-supervised are used when there are few labeled data and a lot of unlabeled data . They thus allow to scale and adopt them to any application for real time text analysis.  For example,   adopted a semi-supervised approach for identifying offensive content in the Twitter corpus. They used the bootstrapping approach to label the unlabeled data based on very small set of generic information. Likewise,   proposed to generate pseudo-labeled documents for training of the model from seed information. It involves the human efforts to just generate the labels and not to label the documents. One of the problems experienced by the semi-supervised techniques is the performance degradation over time as unlabeled data is added to the fixed set of labeled data.

Hybrid text classification approaches

Owing to relative merits and demerits of rule-based and machine learning based text analysis, many researchers have proposed hybrid approaches. For example,   adopted a hybrid approach for the trend analysis of Twitter data. They constructed a topology of trending topics as feature selection and then used automatic classification of tweets to 



appropriate trends. Similarly,   have adopted a hybrid approach by using general concept list as baseline and then constructed ontology based on the collaborative tags by the users. Likewise,   have implemented a hybrid model by combining Naïve Bayes classifier and Support Vector Machine to identify the problems experienced by engineering students from twitter data. They claim that the combination has yielded sufficient reduction in training time and improved accuracy as compared to applying them individually. While hybrid models have been successful in solving particular text classification problem, their performance on large text corpora depends on the test datasets. Therefore, there is no guarantee that the performance achieved on one data set will be same as with any other data set . Moreover, they require several parameters to be defined and initialized in advance. Thus, selecting hybrid classification approach on social data totally depends upon data set and problem being investigated . Likewise, research has suggested few approaches devised around the concept of pooling wherein the selected documents from the given dataset are annotated with relevant concepts by the human experts to create a document collection (pool) of relevant documents for the future referencing, reuse, and other information retrieval related tasks . It implies that once a pool of relevant documents is collected, later some machine learning algorithm can be used to annotate future documents. While such approaches cut off human efforts to some extent by requiring only a subset of documents to be annotated manually; they still require human judgement and tend to be domain specific as they are applied to a single dataset at a time and required topic list to be supplied at the beginning of retrieval process. 

Problem statement and research objectives

Despite the previous research work and substantial efforts of research community as discussed above, the task of real-time text classification of social data is still challenging and an emerging trend. People on social media use irregular data patterns to communicate and do not always use structured sentences, correct grammar and spelling . The use of informal language, character limitations and use of symbols and slang makes the social data analysis a challenging task. Also, the unpresented amount of unstructured data being generated in real time does not allow much time and a well-defined taxonomy to be processed. This presents two interrelated challenges. One, the use of semantical equivalents used by different users. Two, the lower level concepts that can be applied by higher level concepts. For example, the data with ‘ICT’ can be discussed by different users as ICT, information and communication technology, computers, technology, and so on. Further, the data discussing ‘ICT’ can be more related with lower level concepts as ICT implementation, ICT adoption, ICT development, ICT industry, and so on. In addition, the problems presented by user-generated data are multifold including out of dictionary slang words, short forms, punctuations omission, phonetic spellings, and misspellings . Thus, the majority of existing approaches still require some form of labeled data or human annotation in order to perform text classification efficiently and are thus designed for specific type of data and for a particular domain . These approaches designed for a particular domain are not directly implementable to another domain as this is not a fit for all task. For example,   adopted an approach for real time classification of traffic information from social data by leveraging a dictionary of related topics and a lexical analyzer. However, given the dictionary component, the application cannot be adopted to any other domain where a known list of words cannot be determined beforehand. Therefore, there is need to develop more general information processing methods for classification of data pertaining to broad range of data types .



In this realm, this paper presents an approach for real time classification of social data through the corpus-based concept generation and text tagging. The approach is built upon the ideas of feature extraction based on word semantics (as in ), and syntactic features of word . Hence, the proposed approach is capable of addressing the semantical and morphological similarities, and domain-specific vocabularies of query strings and tagged concepts in a general manner. The approach is different from existing approaches in a way that it does not require any dictionary, concept list, or any labeled data beforehand. This makes it adoptable irrespective to any domain and can easily be implemented in any general purpose application relating to the social data retrieval and classification. Wherein, if need be, the application can also be made domain specific just by referencing any suitable corpus related to that particular domain. Also, approach can be applied to any language given the suitable corpora and corpus processing methods/services for that respective language. Thus, to the best of our knowledge, this approach is one of its kinds that addresses the task of social data classification in a general way by offering a hybrid on-the-go method for concept generation and tagging of social data in real-time.

Research Design

The present study has been conducted according to Design Science Research Methodology. DSRM is an applied science methodology which aims at developing technology related innovative artifacts that address real world problems. The resultant artifacts deliver utility as well as novelty . The DSRM employed herein targets to develop a new corpus based text classification and tagging approach for social data. Whilst, there are various models for undertaking DSRM research, this work has been conducted in five steps drawn upon  and ; i.e. problem identification, objective description, solution design, test implementation, and evaluation (cf. Figure 1).

Figure 1- Overall research procedure of the study

In doing so, firstly the problem description was solicited in a problem-driven approach  from the literature review and state of the art of the research area. In this regard, the nature and requirements of classification of social big data was analyzed and compared with existing approaches of text classification. As a result, the shortcomings of 



existing approaches were outlined and a problem statement was defined and specified (cf. Section 2.4). The problem statement was later used to outline the objectives of intended approach that could guide its development and evaluation. Successively, the proposed solution was designed and iteratively improved that can address the identified problem. The solution was designed in light of the identified capabilities of SNS APIs and other existing text similarity and expansion services (cf. Section 4). Successively, a test prototype of a web application that implements the proposed approach was developed to demonstrate the proof-of concept that the proposed approach works to solve the instances of identified problem (cf. Section 5). The approach through its instantiation was evaluated on sample keywords to measure its efficiency and performance. The application was used to generate the concepts and tag the text snippets accordingly which were then evaluated manually by the researchers to determine their relevancy in terms of their precision measure. The evaluation was carried out formatively during the development process with controlled experiments (cf. ) as the application was with the researchers and they themselves carried out the evaluation. The focus of these episodes of formative evaluations was to measure the developed approach against the identified research gap (cf. Section 6). 

Following the DSRM concepts, the resultant approach presented in this paper is designed in iterations. In doing so, a build-and-evaluate loop was exercised with formative evaluation during the development phase to seek the feedback on the proposed approach and determine areas of improvement and refinement. This resulted in re-iteration of the design process. Accordingly, this work consists of three iterations of DSRM process model (cf. Figure 2). The first iteration comprises most of the work presented herein and started by identifying problem and setting solution objectives based on previous literature. Following this, the solution was designed and instantiated wherein the search of relevant data, and concept generation and tagging for the retrieved data was performed only on the basis of keywords given in search query. The resulting first version of proposed approach was then evaluated by the researchers in terms of its response rate, number of concepts generated, and precision of concept tagging. It was then observed that the performance was not good. Thus, the second iteration started from the solution design stage wherein the solution was revised to use original keywords given in search query as well as their semantic equivalents. The implementation and evaluation of this iteration showed that the approach has improved in terms of response rate and number of concepts generated. However, it was revealed that still the approach has low response rate as it was not able to understand similarities between different forms of a word. Therefore, many text snippets having a different form of given keyword were not recognized as relevant and returned by SNS APIs. This informed the third iteration wherein keywords, their semantic equivalents, and morphological forms were also used for text retrieval whereas concept generation and tagging was carried out with the keywords and their semantic equivalents only.

Figure 2 - Iterations of DSRM process model of the study



Proposed Approach

The proposed corpus-based real-time text classification and tagging approach is given in Figure 3. According to the proposed approach, the intended goal of searching the social data and its tagging with relevant two worded and three worded concepts is achieved in five steps of keyword preprocessing, keyword expansion, data search, concept map generation, and data classification and tagging.

Step 1 – Keyword Preprocessing

In the first step, the given string of one or more keywords is cleaned and preprocessed for relevant search. Herein, at first the search string is tokenized and broken into individual keywords. This is achieved through simple whitespace tokenization whereby a string is split into words from whitespaces. Successively the bag of tokenized words (unigrams) is normalized which refers to converting the keywords to their most canonical (standard) form through the process of lemmatization and stemming techniques . This is necessary to address the problems of user-generated data including the slang words, short forms, punctuations omission, phonetic spellings, and misspellings . Normalizing words to their base form helps to reduce the number of unique tokens and remove the word variations and redundancies. The normalized words are then parsed and filtered to discard any stop words. Stop words refer to the most common words which have grammatical function (syntactic in nature more than the semantic) and do not relate more with the actual content . Research shows that removing such words from search keywords improves the performance of information retrieval systems  in terms of efficiency as well as effectiveness . To do so, a stop word list is identified containing both the domain independent stop words (of any specific language) and domain specific stop words (of a particular domain) and then search tokens are matched with these identified stop words through any suitable text matching approach . As a result, the resultant bag of words contains most appropriate, relevant, and standardized tokens that can be used to search the relevant data.

Step 2 – Keyword Expansion

In the second step, the bag of keywords generated in step 1 is semantically and morphologically expanded in order to be able to retrieve more relevant data and improve the information retrieval process. The purpose of semantical expansion of query keywords is to include all other alternate words in the query that might mean the same as the given keywords in order to increase the search recall and precision rates . This is to address the semantical similarities of words in domain in-dependent as well as domain dependent terminologies. Therefore, here the corpus-based semantic word generation and expansion is levied. Using a corpus-based approach helps to capture all similarities (domain specific as well given that domain related literature is referenced) and make the approach and resultant web tool easily adoptable to any domain and any language by just referencing an appropriate domain corpus in a particular language. This results in a collection of actual keywords preprocessed in previous step and their all semantically equivalent words. The resultant bag of words is then processed for morphological extension. Morphological expression refers to different forms of a particular words depending in language lexical that are referred as ‘morphs’ of a particular word. This is necessary as the text similarity measures, especially vector based text similarity approaches, work on exact matching of keywords in the underlying text data sources. Thus, including all forms of a particular word again improves the precision and recall of information retrieval. This results in a bag of words containing 



actual preprocessed keywords, all semantically equivalent words of given keywords, and all morphological forms of keywords and their semantical equivalents. Such that, taking each keyword at a time (say Ki), its semantically similar words (KiS1 to KiSn) are generated, and subsequently all its morphological forms (KiM1 to KiMn) and morphological forms of its semantical equivalents (KiS1M1 to KiSnMn) are generated. This step results into a bag of words (T) for search string with N keywords as T =  i=1 to N (Ki , KiM1 , … , KiMn , KiS1 , …. , KiSn , KiS1M1 , … , KiS1Mn , ……….. , KiSnM1 , … , KiSnMn).

Figure 3 - Proposed corpus-based real-time text classification and tagging approach for social data

Step 3 – Searching Social Data

In the third step, the bag of words generated in the second step is used to query and retrieve the relevant data from respective social networks. For searching social networks, a suitable data search methodology is adopted like the one proposed in . Accordingly, the data is searched through five modules (cf. Figure 4) as 1) query processor module that prepares the semantic search based on given keywords in order to eliminate the vocabulary mismatch problem and improve search recall, 2) query formulator module wherein the query is formulated according to the structure and query format of the respective SNS APIs and proper authentication information is attached to it, 3) data fetcher module that issues repetitive SNS API calls to fetch all relevant data while addressing particular data limits of each API, 4) data mapper module that removes platform dependencies from 



retrieved data and maps all data to a uniform data model, and 5) data integrator model that aggregates all the data retrieved from different SNSs into a single data set while discarding any potential duplicates.

Figure 4 - Social data search procedure based on 

Step 4 – Concept Map Generation

In the fourth step, the actual keywords and their semantic equivalent words are used to generate data concepts. Herein, semantically similar words are used to refer to all relevant words of given keywords. The resultant bag of words is used to identify the left and right co-occurrences of each word (also termed as neighboring terms) based on the semantic analysis of suitable corpus. In this regard, either the domain independent or the domain dependent corpus can be used according to the specific search application. After retrieving the possible left and right neighboring terms of each word, its collations with each left neighboring term (left co-occurrence concept), its each right neighboring term (right co-occurrence concept), and different combinations of left and right neighboring terms (three worded concepts) are created. As a result, taking each keyword at a time (say Wi), its left neighboring terms (Lwi), right neighboring terms (Rwi) are generated. Following this concepts are generated which result into a concept map containing the concepts as i=1 to N (Lwi + Rwi + Lwi*Rwi).

Step 5 – Data Classification and Tagging

For determining the similarity of each concept with the text snippets, the cosine similarity measure is used as it is regarded as most widely used for determining the text similarity  and provides foundation for document classification in many studies . The process of determining the text similarity through cosine similarity measure is to transform each category tag and each text snippets in the retrieved dataset into a vector in some high dimensional space so that we find the similar strings being close to each other. The cosine value of angle shows how similar the two texts are thus represents the similarity of two texts. 

Accordingly, a high dimensional space V is created where each term in each concept in concept map T1 and text snippet T2 defines an independent dimension. The texts are then transformed into their respective binary vectors V1 and V2. With respect to positive quadrant of the Euclidean space wherein no term is assigned a negative value, vector values are used to indicate whether a particular term in each text string is present (1 – identical vector) or not (0 – orthogonal vector). Finally, the cosine of the angle between V1 and V2 is computed which is identical to their normalized inner product, such that Simcos (T1, T2) = 



Implementation

The approach presented in Figure 3 is test implemented in designing a web based application that enables to search for social data from the platform Facebook as well as from application’s native text resources in an aggregate manner. The approach facilitates the synthesis of well-defined query strings by suggesting possible left and right co-occurrences and semantically similar terms. Successively, the application allows searching text resources from connected data servers and then generates concepts and classifies and tags the retrieved text resources accordingly. 

User interface

The initial user interface of the application is divided into 4 parts (cf. Figure 5):

First segment (top panel) provides a search box for entering keywords; as user types in the keyword and hits a blank space signaling the end of a word, the application presents alternative semantically similar terms of given keyword on the top of search box. The user can then select any alternative word instead of given word if found more suitable. In addition to it, as the user finishes a word, the application also suggests the various possible left and right neighboring terms so that the user may add in more relevant collations to the search query. The final search button also to finalize the search string and issue a search command to the server.

Figure 5 - A screenshot of text application implementing proposed approach

Second segment (left panel) presents a concept map generated according to the proposed approach and organized around given search keywords. Besides each concept, the number of resources is indicated which match the particular concept. Clicking on a particular concept allows filtering the result set to show only those resources that match the given concept.

Third segment (middle panel) lists the retrieved resources matching the search query. The resources are organized with respect to different data sources.



Fourth segment (right panel) shows the details of a single selected resource. As an additional function, this part enables the user to interact with the particular resource and thereby like or comment on the retrieved social media post directly within the application.

Technical implementation

The applifaction is configured in a typical three-tier style using a WAMP server architecture. The application is implemented in PHP as server-side scripting language, and HTML and JavaScript with jQuery library as client-side scripting languages using a relational MySQL database for general user management tasks. Dynamic content as per user request is generated through Asynchronous JavaScript calls with JavaScript Object Notation and JavaScript Underscore library is used for results sorting and filtering at the client-dide. The local database is accessed with SQL commands in PHP scripts. The interconnectitvity with the platform Facebook is achieved through Facebook Graph API.

For the morphological expansion of keywords, the application uses PHP based phpMorphy library which is a dictionary-based morphological service for different languages including the English, Russian, German, Ukrainian, and Estonian. The phpMorphy service provides three types of information for each given word which includes the lemma which is the base form of the word, all morphological forms of the word, and grammatical information of the word. Herein, the lemma and morphological forms of the given keyword are retreived and sued by the application.

For the semantical expansion of keywords and during concept generation, the application leverages the corpus-based webservices offered by Leipzig Corpora Collection using its REST API. LCC collects the large corpus using different data collection methods and in different language from the freely available electronic resources such as web, newspapers, and Wikipedia. It processes the collected corpus and calculates a full form dictionary with frequency information for each word. In addition, it precomputes statistics related to the significant co-occurrence/neighboring terms and stores two kinds of co-occurrence information; i.e. words that occur together in sentences, and words that are found as immediate (left or right) neighbors . The LCC collects corpora for different languages and for every language different releases are available. The application references their English language corpus based on Wikipedia with 1M sentences (eng_wikipedia_2012_1M). The complete algorithm for the concept map generation and text snippet tagging is given in listing 1.



Listing 1 - Algorithm for the concept map generation and tagging of text snippets



Evaluation

The designed application has been tested and validated by integrating it into the prototype of a larger collaboration platform that supports interconnectivity among a specific type of organizations throughout the collaboration process. Results of a sample execution of the proposed approach are shown in Figure 6. For the sample execution keyword ‘Innovation’ is used and therein 2-worded and 3-worded concepts are generated to verify the functionality of the application. The application was able to generate 41 two-worded concepts (32 with right co-occurrences and 9 with left co-occurrences) and 288 three-worded concepts.

Figure 6 - Sample concept generation for the keyword 'Innovation’

The proposed approach and the implemented prototype have been evaluated formatively during the continuous design and development cycles. The study adopted a controlled experiment evaluation strategy in an interpretive manner  wherein the researchers served as human evaluators for the different versions of the approach. As discussed in section 3, the resultant approach is devised in three main iterations. At the end of each iteration, the concept map generated by the approach was analysed by 3 researchers to assess the performance and outline the improvements for next iteration. Herein, precision was calculated as the performance indicator. The precision of concept map is defined as the percentage of meaningful concepts out of the total concepts generated such that 

Precision = 



Accordingly, in order to assess the concept map, the concept list generated by the approach was manually checked by the evaluators to judge if a particular concept is related to the given keyword. A particular concept was considered relevant if it is marked relevant by at least two of the evaluators. 

As described in the methodology section, in the first iteration of development, only the given keyword was used to create concepts. The evaluation showed that the performance was good in terms of precision; however, the number of identified concepts was not good as the evaluators could identify several concepts that were not generated by the system. Those unidentified concepts were mainly oriented around semantic equivalents of given keyword. For example, given the word ‘innovation’, the system was able to identify ‘technological innovation’, however, it failed to create a concept as ‘new technology’ while they both are different variants of same concept. This initiated the second iteration whereby the word and its semantic equivalents were used to generate concepts. This resulted in a rise in number of identified concepts but it was observed that there is still room for improvement. For instance, given the keyword ‘computer’, the system was not able to generate concepts involving the word ‘computerized’ despite that it is just another form of the given base term. Thus, in the third iteration of development, the morphological forms of keyword were also used to generate concepts in addition to the word and its semantic equivalents. Contrary to the expectations, this addition increased the number of identified concepts to some extent; it lowered the precision because a higher number of meaningless concepts were created due to different forms of the words. Thus, it was decided to identify concepts based on keyword and semantically equivalent terms only. The test results of each version of the proposed approach for the three keywords are shown in Table 1. 

Keyword Keyword only Keyword + Semantical equivalents (Proposed approach) Keyword + Semantical equivalents + Morphological forms No. of  identified concepts No. of  meaningful concepts Precision (%) No. of  identified concepts No. of  meaningful concepts Precision (%) No. of  identified concepts No. of  meaningful concepts Precision (%) Computer 198 176 88.88 329 287 87.23 412 326 79.12 Business 312 268 85.89 506 435 85.96 598 463 77.42 Development 296 232 78.37 424 312 73.58 534 352 65.91 Average 84.38 % 82.25 % 74.15 % Average number of identified concepts* + 56.20 % + 91.56 % Average precision of concepts* - 2.13 % - 10.23%

Table 1 - Evaluation results of different versions of proposed approach (* as compared to baseline with keyword only)

Subsequently, the accuracy and relevancy of tagged concepts was tested on a set of 3 keywords and for each keyword a set of 3 relevant text documents were used for tagging. The documents and tagged concepts were then manually reviewed by the evaluators to determine the relevancy of tagged concepts and calculate the precision. The precision of tagged concepts is defined as the percentage of relevant tags out of the total number of tags supplied to a partiular text snippet such that 



Precision = 

In doing so, the text segments and the associated tags were reviewed by the evaluators to judge if a particular tagged concept is relevant to the given text. A particular tag was considered relevant if it is marked relevant by at least two of the evaluators. The tests are given in Table 2.

Keyword No. of generated concepts Document No. of tagged concepts No. of correctly tagged concepts Precision (%) 2-worded left concepts 2-worded right concepts 3-worded concepts Total concepts Computer 33 9 297 339 Text 1 315 297 94.28 Text 2 291 272 93.47 Text 3 287 235 81.88 Business 12 38 456 506 Text 1 413 376 91.04 Text 2 487 472 96.91 Text 3 476 447 93.90 Development 24 16 384 424 Text 1 388 339 87.37 Text 2 354 312 88.13 Text 3 397 367 92.44 Average 91.04 %

Table 2 - Evaluation results of concept tagging of the proposed approach

It is important to mention here that the number of identified concepts is highly dependent on the completeness and relatedness of the referenced corpora as is the case with all corpus-based approaches. It is also interesting to note from the results that as number of related concepts increased (by incorporating more synonyms of given keyword and their co-occurrences), the precision of generated concepts decreased. However, this can be controlled by referencing to a good quality and more relevant corpus for identifying the semantically equivalent terms at the beginning of the proposed approach. Another configuration in this regard could be to use of a linguistic dictionary (for general-purpose applications) or a pre-identified synonym list for defined keywords (for domain-specific applications). Nevertheless, as given in Table 1 and Table 2, the proposed approach is able to achieve an average precision of 82.25% for the list of concepts identified and a precision of 91.04% for the concept tags as annotated to particular text segments. Thus, it can be concluded that it is effective in achieving its stated goal of classifying text snippets in real-time.

Conclusion

The current paper has proposed a corpus-based approach for searching and successively categorizing and tagging the social data with relevant concepts in real time. The proposed approach is capable of addressing the semantic and morphological similarities, and domain-specific vocabularies of query string and tagged concepts. The feasibility and application of proposed approach is demonstrated in a web-based tool that allows searching the post from Facebook platform and local data resources of the application and provides search results together with a concept map for further navigation, filtering and refining of search results. The tool has been evaluated by performing multiple search queries and resultant concept tags are analyzed in terms of relevancy and precision in an interpretive manner by the researchers 



themselves in controlled settings. The approach is thereby found effective in achieving its stated goal of classifying text snippets in real-time.

Contributions and Implications

The work presented herein has two contributions which have implications to the theory and practice.

One, the proposed approach adds to the literature on the topics of real-time text classification of social data and corpus-based text classification and tagging approaches. It suggests that how corpus-based hybrid approaches can be designed to achieve the task in real-time without much human effort and processing time. In addition to offering a solution to the identified problem, it opens a new research direction that calls attention from the research community to investigate the task on a deeper level. This would serve to bring new innovative approaches to the literature and also provides the grounds to cross compare, assess, and debate on the utility and effectiveness of different approaches in different contexts. Besides, this work adds to the literature on design science research in a way that it offers an example on how the design science research methodology can suggest and guide the design and development of such approaches. It indicates that how the formative evaluation incorporated during the development of technical artifacts can yield a better understanding of the problem and a suitable solution to the identified problem.

Two, the approach and specifically the developed prototype showcases that how social data can be retrieved through SNS APIs and processed to be leveraged in designing data intensive applications. The application further shows how the corpus-based services can be used to serve different tasks in an on-the-go fashion. Accordingly, this offers the substantial motivation and foundational grounds for the application designers and developers to conceptualize and implement data-intensive application based on social data to solve various real life tasks in real-time. The application developed herein encompassing the proposed approach is intended to serve the end users in the real world towards searching and accessing social data in an effective manner in different contexts. As the approach provides a way to classify textual social data, it can be utilized standalone or incorporated in any other large level web-based platform or natural language processing system dealing with information search and retrieval. When integrated within any system, the approach may aid in achieving a sub task of social data classification and its output (i.e. concept map and tagged data) can then be exploited differently based on the purpose of the enclosing platform.  

Limitations and Outlook

The current study has two limitations which in turn have implications for further research. Firstly, while the approach presented herein substantially offers a solution to the identified problem of real time text classification of social data and opens a new direction for the application developers to conceptualize and implement more such approaches, it is worth to be mentioned that data search and retrieval from social media as proposed in this approach highly relies on the capabilities of SNS APIs and data permissions and is thus vulnerable to their changes in future. Whilst, given the current state of authorization standards of existing SNS APIs and provided that the terms and conditions of their data usage are properly followed, the data can be accessed and leveraged for different purposes. However, there will be a need to continuously update such applications in line of any future changes in SNS data policy. Also, the approach is evaluated with only Facebook data so far and thus there is need to test the approach with data retrieved from other social networks as well in order to demonstrate its utility for varying types of text datasets.



Secondly, the application has been evaluated in isolated manner by the researchers adopting a controlled and interpretive strategy. The aim of the episodes of formative evaluation undertaken during this study was to establish the utility and efficacy of proposed approach by showing how well it performs. While we believe that the approach substantially solves the identified problem, there is still need to deploy it in real world settings so that a summative evaluation on a larger level with real end users can be sought. The feedback of such evaluation will be useful to see its efficacy in broader terms and also indicate how the approach performs over the time for different users with varying search and tagging requirements. Also, the scope of the presented work at the current level was to develop a generic application that can be leveraged in general context irrespective of any domain specificity; it has not been evaluated in terms of any domain specific attributes. Furthermore, to the best of our knowledge, there is no existing approach that can classify text snippets in real time without requiring any sort of predefined list of topics/concepts relating to a particular field. Therefore, it has not been cross compared with other approaches as they all are suitable for respective domains. Thus, adopting the proposed approach to different domains (by referencing to suitable domain-specific corpora) would be beneficial in assessing that if and how the approach performs for the text classification of domain specific datasets. This will also enable to cross-compare the approach with other approaches designed and applied to that particular domain in order to test and showcase its relative utility and effectiveness.

REFERENCES

In der heutigen schnelllebigen Geschäftswelt sind Unternehmen und öffentliche Einrichtungen gefordert, ihre Daten effizient zu nutzen, um wettbewerbsfähig zu bleiben. Das Forschungsprojekt Learn2RAG setzt genau hier an und untersucht die praktischen Mehrwerte von Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). Durch innovative Ansätze der generativen Künstlichen Intelligenz (KI) zielt das Projekt darauf ab, die Nutzung von großen Sprachmodellen (LLMs) für den Mittelstand zu revolutionieren.

Was ist RAG?

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) kombiniert generative KI mit Ihren internen Daten. Anfragen an die KI werden mithilfe hinterlegter Daten ergänzt.

So entstehen präzisere Antworten, die einfacher überprüfbar sind und unternehmensspezifische Kontext-informationen berücksichtigen.

Warum sollten Sie mitmachen?

Haben Sie eine oder mehrere der folgenden Herausforderungen?

Sie würden KI gerne in Ihre Prozesse einbinden, aber die fehlende Nachvollziehbarkeit oder die Möglichkeit von Halluzinationen der KI halten Sie davon ab.

Sie wissen nicht, wie Sie Ihre Daten in die Arbeit mit KI-Systemen integrieren können.

Relevante Informationen liegen in Ihrem Unternehmen verstreut und sind bei Bedarf nicht einsatzbereit verfügbar.

Die Auswahl und Integration passender RAG-Architekturen ist komplex und übersteigt Ihr spezifisches Know-How.

Zeit und Fachkräfte fehlen, um neben dem Tagesgeschäft eigene Prototypen zu entwickeln und RAG-Lösungen zu evaluieren.

Projektziele & Vorgehen

Entwicklung und Evaluierung eines überwachten maschinellen Lernverfahrens zur automatischen Generierung von RAG-Pipelines, die auf spezifische Unternehmensbedürfnisse abgestimmt sind.

Ermöglichung des Zugangs zu generativen KI-Technologien für mindestens 150 Unternehmen, insbesondere für KMUs ohne eigene KI-Expertise.

Bereitstellung einer Open-Source-Plattform, die es Unternehmen ermöglicht, die Vorteile von RAG schnell und kosteneffektiv zu nutzen.

Das Projekt verfolgt einen klaren und strukturierten Ansatz, der von der Forschung über die Entwicklung bis hin zur praktischen Anwendung reicht. Durch interaktive Workshops, Webinare und die Bereitstellung von Open-Source-Ressourcen wird sichergestellt, dass die Ergebnisse nachhaltig und breit genutzt werden können. Ziel ist es, nicht nur die Effizienz in den Unternehmen zu steigern, sondern auch die Wettbewerbsfähigkeit Deutschlands im globalen Kontext zu fördern.

Partner & Beteiligte

Mit einem Gesamtvolumen von über 2.7 Millionen Euro wird das Projekt von einem Konsortium führender wissenschaftlicher Institutionen und Softwareentwickler durchgeführt und durch weitreichende Unternehmensnetzwerke unterstützt.
